title: Tiaki Tinana, Tiaki Wairua: Guardianship of Body and Spirit through Kaitiakitanga
subject: Health & Physical Education
culturalContext: Located within the rohe of Ngāti Kahungunu, Mangakōtukutuku College students will explore the connection between personal hauora (wellbeing) and environmental health through the lens of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku (our local stream). Students will learn how traditional practices of kaitiakitanga (guardianship) directly relate to modern health concepts, drawing on local pūrākau (stories) of the taniwha who protects our waters and the historical significance of the awa as a source of physical and spiritual sustenance for our hapū.
learningObjectives: Identify and explain at least three connections between the health of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku and the hauora of our community using specific local examples
Design and implement a simple water quality testing protocol using both mātauranga Māori observation techniques and scientific measurement tools
Create a personal action plan demonstrating kaitiakitanga by committing to one practice that improves both personal wellbeing and environmental health
Collaboratively develop and present a group waiata or haka that expresses the relationship between tinana (physical health) and wairua (spiritual health) through connection to our awa

activities.title: Whakawhanaungatanga ki te Awa: Connecting to Our Stream
activities.description: Students visit Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku with kaumātua to hear pūrākau of the taniwha protector and learn traditional methods of assessing water health through observation of native species, water clarity, and surrounding vegetation. Students sketch the awa and record observations in their journals.

activities.title: Hei Tātari Wai: Traditional and Scientific Water Testing
activities.description: Using both mātauranga Māori indicators (presence of tuna/eels, kōura/crayfish, watercress) and scientific tools (pH strips, thermometer, turbidity tube), students conduct water quality assessments at three points along the awa and compare results to historical data provided by local environmental monitors.

activities.title: Hauora Whakapapa: Mapping Health Connections
activities.description: Students create visual maps showing how the health of the awa connects to their own wellbeing - from drinking water and kai sources to recreational swimming and spiritual connection. They interview whānau members about historical uses of the awa for healing and sustenance.

activities.title: Mahi Tahi: Community Action Project
activities.description: Working with local kaitiaki, students participate in a riparian planting day along the banks of Te Awa o Mangakōtukutuku, learning about native plants that filter water and prevent erosion while discussing how environmental action contributes to collective wellbeing.

activities.title: Whakapuaki: Expressing Our Kaitiakitanga
activities.description: In small groups, students create and perform original waiata, haka, or visual art pieces that express their understanding of the connection between caring for the awa and maintaining personal hauora, presenting these to whānau and community members.

resources: Ngāti Kahungunu kaumātua and local water kaitiaki as cultural experts
Te Awa Tupua: Environmental monitoring data from Hawke's Bay Regional Council
'He Kohikohinga Wai Māori: Traditional Māori Water Collection Methods' (local resource book)
Water testing kits from the National Institute of Water and Atmospheric Research (NIWA) Stream Health Monitoring Guide

tasks: Journal entries documenting observations and reflections from awa visits
Completed water quality testing data sheets with comparative analysis
Personal kaitiakitanga action plan with measurable goals
Group cultural performance and self-assessment rubric

nzcAlignment: Health and Physical Education: Strand A - Personal Health and Physical Development (A1, A3)
Health and Physical Education: Strand D - Healthy Communities and Environments (D1, D3)
Social Sciences: Place and Environment - Understand how people perceive, represent, and interact with places and environments
Tikanga ā-Iwi: Understand how cultural practices reflect and express people's customs, traditions, and values
